question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user submit requests with gas <= 10000 (below GAS_SCALE_FACTOR) so that scaleGas() returns 0 and gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(0) always succeeds, allowing unlimited gas-free requests that flood the node and reorder pending transaction processing order?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential check order in throttle()—where rateLimitBucket token is consumed before gasLimitBucket is checked—to cause rateLimitBucket tokens to be permanently lost when gasLimitBucket.tryConsume() fails, starving legitimate users and altering the effective transaction ordering seen by the node?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger repeated InvalidParametersException paths in ContractController.call() to cause throttleManager.restore(request.getGas()) to be called multiple times, artificially inflating gasLimitBucket tokens beyond capacity and allowing subsequent high-gas requests to bypass throttling and disrupt transaction history ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with estimate=true to bypass the restoreGasToBucket() call in ContractCallService, consuming gas tokens without restoration and selectively starving the gasLimitBucket to delay or reorder other users' transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the opcodeRateLimitBucket by rapidly calling the opcode endpoint, exhausting the single-token-per-second limit and preventing legitimate opcode-based historical queries from executing, effectively reorganizing which historical states are observable?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a request with the maximum allowed gas value, trigger an InvalidParametersException after throttle() succeeds, and cause restore() to add back a large number of tokens to gasLimitBucket, enabling a subsequent burst of high-gas requests that overwhelm the node and disrupt transaction history sequencing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket does not use SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED (unlike gasLimitBucket) to create a race condition where concurrent requests all pass the rateLimitBucket check simultaneously, bypassing the per-second request limit and flooding the node to affect transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set the block field in ContractCallRequest to a historical block value to bypass BLOCK-based RequestFilter rules configured with FilterType.EQUALS on 'latest', avoiding REJECT or THROTTLE actions and submitting unlimited historical-block queries that pollute the node's transaction history view?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a data field that contains a substring matching a CONTAINS-type RequestFilter expression to intentionally trigger the LOG action, causing sensitive ContractCallRequest details (from, to, gas, data) to be written to logs and leak information about other users' transaction patterns?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the probabilistic nature of RequestProperties.test() (which uses RandomUtils.secure().randomLong(0L, 100L) >= rate for non-THROTTLE actions) to statistically bypass REJECT filters by sending many requests, with some passing through and disrupting transaction history ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the counter.getAndIncrement() >= limit check in RequestProperties.test() where the counter increments even when the limit is exceeded, causing the AtomicLong counter to overflow to Long.MIN_VALUE after Long.MAX_VALUE increments, resetting the limit check and re-enabling REJECT filters that were previously exhausted?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the restore() path with gas=0 (which scaleGas() maps to 0 tokens, skipping addTokens()) repeatedly to create a no-op restore loop that masks actual bucket state, making it appear throttling is active when it is not, and allowing other concurrent requests to slip through?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set gas=21000 (the minimum allowed by @Min(21_000)) which scaleGas() maps to 2 tokens (21000/10000=2), and submit thousands of such minimal-gas requests to slowly drain the gasLimitBucket while consuming minimal rate-limit tokens, starving legitimate high-gas transactions and altering their execution order?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the RequestFilter uses case-insensitive CONTAINS matching (Strings.CI::contains) on the data field to craft hex-encoded calldata that matches a filter expression regardless of case, bypassing or triggering filter actions to manipulate which transactions are throttled or rejected?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user submit requests with the FROM field set to the zero address (0x0000...0000) to bypass the hasFrom() validation (which only requires from != null when value > 0), pass throttle() without triggering FROM-based filters, and execute contract calls that appear to originate from the zero address in historical records?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the greedy refill strategy of rateLimitBucket (refillGreedy with capacity=requestsPerSecond) to burst all available tokens at the start of each second, submitting a concentrated burst of requests that overwhelm the node's processing queue and reorder pending transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of an upper bound check in restore() (which calls gasLimitBucket.addTokens(tokens) without capping at bucket capacity) to add tokens beyond the bucket's configured capacity, allowing subsequent requests to consume more gas than the per-second limit should permit and disrupting fair transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user set estimate=true in ContractCallRequest to invoke the ETH_ESTIMATE_GAS path, which skips the restoreGasToBucket() call in ContractCallService.doProcessCall(), permanently consuming gas tokens from gasLimitBucket without restoration and starving real ETH_CALL transactions from executing in their expected order?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that RequestProperties.test() iterates filters with OR semantics (returning true if ANY filter matches) to craft a request that matches one of multiple configured filters, triggering unintended THROTTLE or REJECT actions meant for different request patterns and disrupting other users' transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the empty filters list behavior in RequestProperties.test() (which returns true when filters.isEmpty()) to trigger action() on every request when no filters are configured, causing all requests to be subject to LOG/REJECT/THROTTLE actions and disrupting normal transaction processing order?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests that all pass rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1) before any of them reach gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(), exploiting the non-atomic two-bucket check to allow more requests than intended per second, flooding the node and reordering transaction execution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the restore() call from ContractController.call()'s catch block by submitting a request with gas just above evmProperties.getMaxGasLimit() to cause InvalidParametersException, then restore() adds back the full gas amount, effectively making the throttle check a no-op for that request slot and freeing capacity for subsequent requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GAS_SCALE_FACTOR=10000 boundary in scaleGas() by submitting requests with gas=10001 (which maps to 1 token) versus gas=10000 (which maps to 0 tokens), using the 0-token requests to bypass gas throttling entirely and submit unlimited requests that affect transaction history ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a data field containing a very long hex string that matches a CONTAINS filter expression, causing the filter evaluation to be computationally expensive and slowing down throttle() processing, creating a timing window where other requests bypass throttle checks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the LOG action in action() calls log.info('{}', request) which serializes the entire ContractCallRequest, to craft a request with an extremely large data field that causes excessive memory allocation during logging and degrades node performance, indirectly reordering transaction processing?
